title: Green Discharge in Pregnant Dogs: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Seeing green discharge from your pregnant dog? Learn why this is a high-priority emergency and when to contact a veterinarian immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Green discharge in a pregnant dog is a critical emergency if a puppy is not delivered within 30 to 60 minutes, as it indicates placental detachment. Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if your dog is not actively in labor or the birthing process has stalled.

What does green discharge mean in a pregnant dog?

In pregnant dogs, a dark green discharge is caused by a pigment called uteroverdin. This pigment is released when the placenta separates from the uterine wall. While it is a natural part of the birthing process, its timing is the most important factor in determining the health of the mother and the puppies.

Is green discharge in my pregnant dog a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for green discharge is High.
  • If your dog is not actively in labor or if it has been more than 30 to 60 minutes since the green discharge appeared without a puppy being born, it is a critical emergency.
  • This indicates that at least one placenta has detached, meaning the puppy is no longer receiving oxygen and must be delivered immediately to survive.
  • Please contact an emergency veterinarian right away.

Why should I take a photo of my dog's discharge for triage?

  • If you are unsure whether what you are seeing is normal, taking a clear photo of the discharge and the vulva can be life-saving.
  • A photo allows a veterinary professional to quickly assess the color, volume, and consistency of the fluid.
  • This helps them distinguish between normal labor progression and signs of infection or fetal distress, ensuring your dog receives the correct level of care immediately.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

During normal parturition, vaginal discharge can be clear, serous to hemorrhagic, or green (uteroverdin). However, a dark green vaginal discharge before the delivery of any neonates indicates placental separation and warrants immediate veterinary attention. Dystocia should be suspected in cases of abnormal vulvar discharge, among other signs. The cause of dystocia (obstructive vs. non-obstructive) must be determined, and the animal's overall condition should be assessed to determine appropriate therapy.

Chapter: Reproduction

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2227)

faqs.question: What if my dog is acting normal but has green discharge?
faqs.answer: Even if your dog seems comfortable, green discharge without the birth of a puppy is a sign of placental separation. This is always a medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary evaluation.

faqs.question: Is green discharge ever normal?
faqs.answer: It is only considered normal if a puppy is delivered within minutes of the discharge appearing. If the discharge occurs before labor starts or between puppies without a birth, it is a cause for concern.

faqs.question: What should I do on the way to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog calm and horizontal in the car. If possible, have someone sit with her to monitor for the birth of a puppy during transport, and bring clean towels just in case.
